subject: left-handed people
one_liner: A games library mirrored along the vertical axis, so the action finally happens on the correct side of the screen.
brand_voice_notes: Deadpan hardware-company confidence. Verso talks about mirroring 41,000 games the way a storage vendor talks about redundancy: as a solved engineering problem with a published SLA. Every obviously broken outcome (backwards subtitles, unfinishable tutorials, a webcam that cannot tell which hand is which) is reported as a specification, never an apology. Numbers are stated to false precision. The company is certain, unhurried, and slightly menacing about handedness. Never winks. Never jokes about itself. The catalogue is flipped, the credits roll backwards, and Verso considers the matter closed.

hero.sub: Verso mirrors all 41,208 titles along the vertical axis in real time, so left-handed players see the action on the correct side of the screen. The subtitles are mirrored as well. We have reviewed this and classified it as fidelity.

features.title: Axis Inversion
features.blurb: Every title in the catalogue renders flipped at the driver level. Menus, road signs, subtitles, and the end credits all come out backwards. Three engineers filed this as a defect. It has been reclassified as authenticity and closed.

features.title: Dominance Attestation
features.blurb: A 40-second webcam ritual establishes which hand you actually favour before checkout. Right-handed accounts retain full browsing rights. They may wishlist. They may not purchase. There is no appeals process, because there is nothing to appeal.

features.title: Keybind Reflection
features.blurb: WASD is reflected across the keyboard's center line and lands on OKL semicolon. No player has completed a tutorial since March. On paper the ergonomics are flawless, and we have the paper.

pricing_tiers.name: Portside
perks: One handedness attestation per calendar year
Community forums, read right to left

pricing_tiers.name: Cack-Handed
pricing_tiers.tagline: Our most popular tier. We have not audited why.
perks: Priority placement in the asset reversal queue
Mirrored achievements, which do not sync anywhere
One physical mirror, shipped quarterly, unrequested

pricing_tiers.name: Sinister
pricing_tiers.tagline: For households with more than one.
perks: Up to six confirmed left hands per residence
A dedicated handedness auditor, on retainer
Right-handed relatives receive read-only guest passes
Quarterly review of your household's dominant side

testimonials.quote: I beat a boss for the first time in nine years. It was the wrong boss, in a level I had already cleared, but it was on the correct side of the screen and I felt seen.
testimonials.name: Gareth Umlauf
testimonials.title: Left-Handed Since Birth, Attested Tier II

testimonials.quote: The attestation flagged me as ambidextrous and froze my library for eleven days. Support was warm about it. They sent a mirror.
testimonials.name: Marguerite Oyelaran
testimonials.title: Regional Coordinator of Ergonomic Compliance, Second Shift

testimonials.quote: Every subtitle in my library is backwards, so I have started reading in a mirror. My comprehension is up. My marriage is not.
testimonials.name: Dev Rasmussen
testimonials.title: Retired Competitive Mirror-Mode Speedrunner

demo_screen_concept: The Verso desktop client, mirrored: a game launcher whose entire chrome sits on the wrong side. Navigation rail is pinned to the RIGHT edge (Library / Store / Reversal Queue / Attestation), the library grid of 9 fake games sits left of it, and every card carries a Southpaw Compatibility score out of 100 plus a status badge (MIRRORED / UNFLIPPABLE / PENDING REVERSAL). A master MIRROR toggle in the top chrome flips the entire client along the vertical axis live, in front of the user, with a 400ms transform. A filter row (All / Mirrored / Unflippable / Pending) and a working fake search filter the grid. Clicking a card opens a store detail panel with the featured title's key art, a Southpaw score breakdown, and an INSTALL button that drops the game into a Reversal Queue where every download is permanently stuck at 'reversing assets 47%'. A 'Verify Handedness' button opens a modal that runs a fake 3-second webcam attestation with a scanning progress bar and always returns the verdict INCONCLUSIVE, offering only a 'Try again' button.

images.rationale: Hero skipped because the hero's focal object is an inset screen showing the mirrored launcher client behind a debossed plastic bezel, which is CSS-drawn; a photograph would fight the molded chassis and duplicate the demo. Demo got one asset: the store detail panel is photo-shaped (game key art is album-art-shaped), so the featured title carries real painted key art. The remaining budget went to the three testimonial headshots, shot as deadpan corporate ID-badge portraits of attested left-handers. 4 of 4 images used, all passed the vision judge on the first attempt.

items.prompt: Painterly cinematic video-game cover key art, digital matte painting. A lone armored knight in a weathered cloak stands on a rocky cliff edge, seen from behind in silhouette, facing LEFT toward a vast storm-lit valley. Heavy charcoal (#2B2F36) storm clouds, a single break of muted vermilion (#C43A26) light on the horizon, cold greige (#DCD6C8) mist pooling in the valley below. Dramatic, moody, high-budget fantasy action game marketing art. One focal subject, strong silhouette, cinematic composition. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens, no title treatment.

Deadpan corporate ID-badge headshot, editorial photograph. A middle-aged man, unsmiling, shoulders square to camera, head and shoulders centered. Plain warm ABS-plastic greige backdrop (#DCD6C8), flat even studio lighting, charcoal (#2B2F36) collared shirt. Tired around the eyes, the flat expression of a man who has been asked to prove which hand he favours. 85mm lens, neutral color grade, one muted vermilion (#C43A26) lanyard strap as the only saturated element. One focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate ID-badge headshot, editorial photograph. A woman in her forties with locs pulled back, unsmiling, composed, faintly impatient, head and shoulders centered, shoulders square to camera. Plain warm ABS-plastic greige backdrop (#DCD6C8), flat even studio lighting, charcoal (#2B2F36) blazer. The bureaucratic calm of a compliance officer who works the second shift. 85mm lens, neutral color grade, one muted vermilion (#C43A26) lanyard strap as the only saturated element. One focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate ID-badge headshot, editorial photograph. A gaunt man in his early thirties with shaggy hair and faint dark circles, unsmiling, staring slightly past the lens, head and shoulders centered. Plain warm ABS-plastic greige backdrop (#DCD6C8), flat even studio lighting, a plain charcoal (#2B2F36) t-shirt. The thousand-yard stare of a retired competitive speedrunner. 85mm lens, neutral color grade, one muted vermilion (#C43A26) lanyard strap as the only saturated element. One focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

design_direction.style: Skeuomorphic hardware-chassis storefront, molded-plastic daylight mode. The page is staged as the product page of a games company that ships its storefront as though it were a physical console, and that has mirrored the entire chassis because its users are left-handed. A warm ABS-plastic greige ground carries molded panels: beveled surfaces with 12px radii, a 1px top highlight and a soft lower shadow so every card reads as injection-molded polycarbonate rather than paper, with visible Phillips screw heads set into the corners of major panels and thin recessed seams running between sections. Buttons are physically chunky, with a 3px darker bottom bevel that compresses to 1px on press so the control genuinely depresses under the cursor. Screens (the hero product plate, the demo's viewport) are inset behind a debossed bezel with a faint scanline wash and a subtle inner shadow, like glass sunk into plastic. The mirror is the whole conceit: the nav sits on the RIGHT, the hero copy is right-aligned and flows from the right edge, feature panels are numbered in reverse (03, 02, 01), and every directional arrow in the layout points left. Body text stays left-to-right and readable, which is exactly the point, because Verso mirrored everything it could reach and stopped there. One charcoal chassis ink carries the wordmark, headlines, molded labels, and all structural text; one rationed arcade vermilion, the color of a physical face button, is reserved strictly for the single primary CTA per section, the attestation status lamp, and the MIRROR toggle when engaged, and appears almost nowhere else. Russo One sets the wordmark and headlines in wide single-weight console-hardware caps, the typeface of a molded logo badge; Chakra Petch carries angular, faintly technical body copy. Every figure that pretends to be platform telemetry (Southpaw Compatibility scores, catalogue counts, reversal percentages, tier prices, attestation durations) is set in Share Tech Mono with tabular figures inside recessed label wells, so it reads like a spec sticker on the underside of a device, not marketing. Motion is mechanical and short: buttons depress on press, the status lamp pulses on a 3s cycle, panels rise 6px and fade in on scroll, and the MIRROR toggle flips its target along the vertical axis with a 400ms ease, all gated behind prefers-reduced-motion. No dark mode, no glass, no blur veils, no neon, no clay, no bento lattice, no marquee, no serifs, no paper grain. Reads like a hardware company that solved handedness by turning the entire product around and has not yet been told what that did to the text.

fonts.body: 'Chakra Petch', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif

design_direction.archetype: Gaming storefront and launcher landing, Steam-adjacent, staged as console hardware packaging and mirrored end to end: right-hand nav with a molded wordmark badge, hero with right-aligned copy and an inset product screen showing the mirrored client, features as three molded chassis panels numbered in reverse, testimonials as attested-user cards with headshots, pricing as three hardware tiers, a vermilion attestation CTA band, and a handoff to the demo client.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Mechanical and short. Chunky buttons compress their 3px bottom bevel to 1px on :active so controls physically depress; the attestation status lamp pulses on a 3s cycle; molded panels rise 6px and fade in once on scroll via IntersectionObserver; the MIRROR toggle flips its target with a 400ms ease transform; hero screen scanlines drift almost imperceptibly. Nothing bounces, nothing parallaxes, everything gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Right-aligned hero copy occupying the right column, with the molded VERSO wordmark badge and the nav rail pinned to the right edge so the whole page reads reversed on first load. Left column holds an inset screen: a debossed bezel with a faint scanline wash, showing a miniature of the mirrored launcher client with its nav rail on the wrong side and a game grid behind it. A Share Tech Mono spec strip runs under the screen with catalogue count, mirrored percentage, and attestation latency in recessed label wells. The single vermilion CTA (Verify your hand) sits under the hero copy with a chunky 3px bottom bevel; a small vermilion status lamp pulses beside the attestation counter. Phillips screw heads sit in the four corners of the hero chassis panel.
